card.intuition: Two-pass DFS: Pass 1 runs DFS on the original graph and records finish times; Pass 2 runs DFS on the transposed graph in decreasing finish-time order. Each DFS tree in Pass 2 is one SCC.
card.analogy: If you can reach someone in a group AND they can reach you back (via the original roads), you're in the same strongly-connected town. Reversing all roads and exploring in 'last-finished-first' order ensures you stay within your own town.
card.time_complexity: O(V + E) — two full DFS passes
card.space_complexity: O(V + E) for the transposed graph
when_to_use: Finding SCCs when you prefer two clear passes over Tarjan's single-pass complexity
When the transposed graph is easy to build (e.g., already have reverse edge lists)
Educational contexts — the two-pass intuition is often easier to explain
gotchas: Pass 1 uses the ORIGINAL graph; Pass 2 uses the TRANSPOSED (reversed-edge) graph
Process nodes in DECREASING finish order in Pass 2 — use a stack pushed on node finish
Both passes need a visited set — reset between passes

hints: Pass 2 explores the transposed graph (radj) — iterate over radj[u] to follow reversed edges
exercise.test_code:
adj = {0:[1], 1:[2,3], 2:[0], 3:[]}
sccs = kosarajus_scc(4, adj)
assert len(sccs) == 2
assert [0,1,2] in sccs
assert [3] in sccs

adj2 = {0:[1],1:[2],2:[]}
sccs2 = kosarajus_scc(3, adj2)
assert len(sccs2) == 3
assert all(len(s)==1 for s in sccs2)
print('PASS')
